news_summary: Significant advances in human rights include the dismantling of racist voting bans in US states, France abolishing its last slavery law, historic progress on disability rights at the UN, and increased global efforts to combat child labor and raise awareness on humanitarian issues.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées majeures en matière de droits de l'homme, on peut citer la suppression des interdictions de vote fondées sur la race dans certains États américains, l'abrogation par la France de sa dernière loi sur l'esclavage, les progrès historiques réalisés aux Nations unies en faveur des droits des personnes handicapées, ainsi que l'intensification des efforts mondiaux pour lutter contre le travail des enfants et sensibiliser le public aux questions humanitaires.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances significativos en materia de derechos humanos cabe destacar la eliminación de las prohibiciones racistas del derecho al voto en algunos estados de EE. UU., la derogación por parte de Francia de su última ley sobre la esclavitud, los avances históricos en materia de derechos de las personas con discapacidad en la ONU y el aumento de los esfuerzos a nivel mundial para combatir el trabajo infantil y sensibilizar sobre cuestiones humanitarias.

articles.title: US States Dismantle Voting Bans Rooted in Racism
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Maryland Governor Wes Moore (center) along with advocates, organizers, and legislators after signing HB 115 and SB 241 at the Maryland State House in Annapolis, May 12, 2026.
                    © 2026 Maryland GovPics
          
    


  
In recent weeks, several states have taken steps towards restoring the voting rights of people with felony convictions. Governor Wes Moore of Maryland signed on May 12 HB 115 and SB 241, legislation that will automatically register people to vote following the completion of their felony sentence. Similarly, the Missouri legislature passed on May 28 HB 1871, a bill that will restore voting rights for some individuals completing felony parole or probation. That bill awaits Governor Mike Kehoe’s signature.These are strides forward on an issue impacting 4.4 million Americans, the overwhelming majority of whom are Black or Latino. But significant work remains to extend the right to vote to all, regardless of their encounters with the criminal legal system.In the United States, felony disenfranchisement laws serve among other things as relics of a past defined by racial hierarchy. After the collapse of Reconstruction, white supremacist groups’ terror campaigns, and policies aimed at excluding Black voters, rose in tandem. States implemented poll taxes, grandfather clauses, and literacy tests at the same time the modern-day US prison system was growing due to Black Codes, laws that targeted newly freed Black Americans. These policies, including felony disenfranchisement, have been credited with making way for Jim Crow, a legal order based on white supremacy that was akin to a caste system and lasted nearly a century in some states. While the nearly-destroyed 1965 Voting Rights Act outlawed many of these suppressive policies, the disenfranchisement of those with felony convictions remains law in 48 states.Today, 1 in 22 Black Americans of voting age is disenfranchised, according to The Sentencing Project. The numbers are even worse in former slave-holding states: In Florida, nearly one million residents are disenfranchised—the largest disenfranchised population in the United States. In Tennessee, a state that recently made headlines for eliminating its only majority-Black congressional district, nearly 1 in 5 Black Americans cannot vote due to felony convictions.Access to the ballot is a human right and foundational to a functioning democracy. While the recent victories in Missouri and Maryland represent a step in the right direction, all states which have felony disenfranchisement laws should reverse them and ensure that every American has the right to vote.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Maryland Governor Wes Moore (center) along with advocates, organizers, and legislators after signing HB 115 and SB 241 at the Maryland State House in Annapolis, May 12, 2026. © 2026 Maryland GovPics

In recent weeks, several states have taken steps towards restoring the voting rights of people with felony convictions. Governor Wes Moore of Maryland signed on May 12 HB 115 and SB 241, legislation that will automatically register people to vote following the completion of their felony sentence. Similarly, the Missouri legislature passed on May 28 HB 1871, a bill that will restore voting rights for some individuals completing felony parole or probation. That bill awaits Governor Mike Kehoe’s signature.

These are strides forward on an issue impacting 4.4 million Americans, the overwhelming majority of whom are Black or Latino. But significant work remains to extend the right to vote to all, regardless of their encounters with the criminal legal system.

In the United States, felony disenfranchisement laws serve among other things as relics of a past defined by racial hierarchy. After the collapse of Reconstruction, white supremacist groups’ terror campaigns, and policies aimed at excluding Black voters, rose in tandem. States implemented poll taxes, grandfather clauses, and literacy tests at the same time the modern-day US prison system was growing due to Black Codes, laws that targeted newly freed Black Americans. These policies, including felony disenfranchisement, have been credited with making way for Jim Crow, a legal order based on white supremacy that was akin to a caste system and lasted nearly a century in some states. While the nearly-destroyed 1965 Voting Rights Act outlawed many of these suppressive policies, the disenfranchisement of those with felony convictions remains law in 48 states.

Today, 1 in 22 Black Americans of voting age is disenfranchised, according to The Sentencing Project. The numbers are even worse in former slave-holding states: In Florida, nearly one million residents are disenfranchised—the largest disenfranchised population in the United States. In Tennessee, a state that recently made headlines for eliminating its only majority-Black congressional district, nearly 1 in 5 Black Americans cannot vote due to felony convictions.

Access to the ballot is a human right and foundational to a functioning democracy. While the recent victories in Missouri and Maryland represent a step in the right direction, all states which have felony disenfranchisement laws should reverse them and ensure that every American has the right to vote.
articles.reasoning: The article reports meaningful legislative progress in restoring voting rights to millions of Americans with felony convictions, addressing a significant racial justice issue with broad societal impact. The story provides detailed context on the historical and systemic nature of felony disenfranchisement and highlights recent concrete policy changes in Maryland and Missouri, demonstrating real-world positive change toward equality and democracy.
articles.personality_title: Maryland and Missouri restore voting rights for people with felony convictions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, many people with felony convictions lose their right to vote. This rule, called felony disenfranchisement, affects over 4 million Americans and mostly impacts Black and Latino communities. These laws have a long history tied to racial discrimination and were part of efforts to keep Black Americans from voting after the Civil War.

**What happened** – Recently, Maryland and Missouri passed new laws to restore voting rights to people with felony convictions. On May 12, Maryland’s Governor Wes Moore signed bills HB 115 and SB 241, which will automatically register people to vote once they finish their felony sentences. Missouri’s legislature approved HB 1871 on May 28, which will restore voting rights for some people after parole or probation. This bill is waiting for Governor Mike Kehoe’s signature.

**Impact** – These laws help millions of Americans regain their voice in elections. For example, in Florida, nearly one million people are currently unable to vote because of felony convictions. Restoring voting rights can improve fairness in elections and reduce racial inequalities. It also challenges old laws rooted in unfair treatment of Black Americans.

**What's next step** – Although these changes are important, most states still have laws that stop people with felony convictions from voting. More states can follow Maryland and Missouri by changing their rules. Advocates hope all states will eventually allow every citizen to vote, no matter their past.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Maryland and Missouri have taken important steps to restore voting rights to people with felony convictions, helping to make elections fairer and more inclusive.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Maryland et le Missouri rétablissent le droit de vote aux personnes condamnées pour un crime
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, de nombreuses personnes condamnées pour un crime perdent leur droit de vote. Cette règle, appelée privation des droits civiques pour les criminels, concerne plus de 4 millions d’Américains, principalement des communautés noires et latino-américaines. Ces lois ont une longue histoire liée à la discrimination raciale, visant à empêcher les Afro-Américains de voter après la guerre civile.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, le Maryland et le Missouri ont adopté de nouvelles lois pour rétablir le droit de vote aux personnes condamnées. Le 12 mai, le gouverneur du Maryland, Wes Moore, a signé les projets de loi HB 115 et SB 241, qui inscriront automatiquement les personnes sur les listes électorales après la fin de leur peine. Le 28 mai, la législature du Missouri a approuvé le projet HB 1871, qui restaurera le droit de vote à certaines personnes après leur libération conditionnelle ou probation. Ce projet attend la signature du gouverneur Mike Kehoe.

**Impact** – Ces lois permettent à des millions d’Américains de retrouver leur voix lors des élections. Par exemple, en Floride, près d’un million de personnes ne peuvent pas voter à cause de condamnations. Rétablir ce droit contribue à plus d’équité dans les élections et réduit les inégalités raciales. Cela remet aussi en question des lois anciennes basées sur un traitement injuste des Afro-Américains.

**Prochaine étape** – Bien que ces changements soient importants, la plupart des États maintiennent encore des lois qui empêchent les personnes condamnées de voter. D’autres États peuvent suivre l’exemple du Maryland et du Missouri en modifiant leurs règles. Les défenseurs espèrent que tous les États permettront un jour à chaque citoyen de voter, quel que soit son passé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Maryland et le Missouri ont fait un pas important en restaurant le droit de vote aux personnes condamnées, rendant les élections plus justes et inclusives.
articles.personality_title_es: Maryland y Misuri restauran el derecho a voto a personas con condenas penales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, muchas personas con condenas penales pierden su derecho a votar. Esta regla, llamada privación del derecho al voto por delitos graves, afecta a más de 4 millones de estadounidenses, principalmente a comunidades negras y latinas. Estas leyes tienen una larga historia ligada a la discriminación racial y fueron usadas para impedir que los afroamericanos votaran después de la Guerra Civil.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, Maryland y Misuri aprobaron nuevas leyes para devolver el derecho a voto a personas con condenas penales. El 12 de mayo, el gobernador de Maryland, Wes Moore, firmó los proyectos de ley HB 115 y SB 241, que registrarán automáticamente a las personas para votar después de terminar su condena. El 28 de mayo, la legislatura de Misuri aprobó HB 1871, que restaurará el derecho a voto a algunas personas tras cumplir libertad condicional o supervisión. Esta ley espera la firma del gobernador Mike Kehoe.

**Impacto** – Estas leyes ayudan a millones de estadounidenses a recuperar su voz en las elecciones. Por ejemplo, en Florida, cerca de un millón de personas no pueden votar debido a condenas penales. Restaurar el derecho a voto puede mejorar la justicia en las elecciones y reducir desigualdades raciales. También desafía leyes antiguas basadas en un trato injusto hacia los afroamericanos.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque estos cambios son importantes, la mayoría de los estados aún tienen leyes que impiden votar a personas con condenas penales. Más estados pueden seguir el ejemplo de Maryland y Misuri cambiando sus normas. Los defensores esperan que todos los estados permitan algún día que todos los ciudadanos voten, sin importar su pasado.

**Resumen en una frase** – Maryland y Misuri han dado pasos importantes para restaurar el derecho a voto a personas con condenas penales, haciendo las elecciones más justas e inclusivas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of diverse hands unlocking and opening a large, ornate ballot box shaped like a cage, with broken chains falling away and vibrant rays of soft natural colors spreading outward, symbolizing the restoration of voting rights to formerly disenfranchised individuals.

articles.title: France finally votes to strike the Code Noir from its books, its last slavery law
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM There is something disquieting about the idea that a law governing slavery could still exist on the books in 2026. Not as an enforced law. Not as a policy. Just sitting there, formally unrepealed, in the archive of French legal history. That was the status of the Code Noir […]
The post France finally votes to strike the Code Noir from its books, its last slavery law first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

There is something disquieting about the idea that a law governing slavery could still exist on the books in 2026. Not as an enforced law. Not as a policy. Just sitting there, formally unrepealed, in the archive of French legal history.

That was the status of the Code Noir until May 28, 2026, when France’s National Assembly voted to annul it. The margin was 254 to zero.

The Code Noir was signed by King Louis XIV in 1685. It governed enslaved people in France’s colonies: classifying them as moveable goods that could be inherited, requiring Catholic conversion, and prescribing ear mutilation for escape attempts. France abolished slavery in 1848 and declared it a crime against humanity in 2001. In all that time, nobody touched the law.

What the vote means, and what it does not

The unanimous National Assembly vote was one step. The Senate still needs to approve the repeal before it becomes law. President Macron said he supported it the week before the vote.

Max Mathiasin, the Guadeloupe lawmaker who championed the bill, cried when his colleagues raised their hands. Steevy Gustave, a Greens lawmaker whose father was born in Martinique, put it plainly from the floor: “We are not descendants of slaves. We are descendants of human beings who were born free, then reduced to slavery.”

The language matters. The vote was called symbolic from the start, and opponents of that framing were not quiet about it.

The louder argument underneath

Repealing an obsolete law is relatively easy. Reparations are not.

Activist Dieudonne Boutrin said it plainly: the repeal “changes nothing. Black people are still looked at the same way.” He and others called for a formal reparations programme addressing educational inequity and systemic racism.

Martinique official Serge Letchimy went further, citing “lasting historical, cultural, social, economic and psychological harm” and pointing to a 10-point plan from Caribbean nations that includes debt cancellation and healthcare investment.

Haiti is the clearest case. After winning independence in 1804, the country was forced to pay France hundreds of millions of francs, reparations to its former colonizers for the loss of enslaved labor. Haiti took high-interest loans to cover the payments and only cleared the debt in 1952.

A country still sorting out what happened

France was Europe’s third-largest slave trader, after Britain and Portugal. Between the 17th and 19th centuries, more than one million Africans were transported into slavery on French ships, most of them bound for Caribbean plantations.

The unanimous vote last week was not nothing. It closed a legal gap that should have been closed in 1848. But the lawmakers who wept in the chamber and the activists outside appear to be arguing about two different things: one a matter of record, the other still unresolved.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on France's National Assembly unanimously voting to repeal the Code Noir, the last law governing slavery still on the books, symbolizing a formal legal and moral reckoning with its colonial past. This act has broad societal significance, acknowledging historical injustices and advancing human rights, with detailed context about the law's history and ongoing calls for reparations and systemic change.
articles.personality_title: France's National Assembly votes to repeal last slavery law, the Code Noir
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Code Noir was a law created in 1685 by King Louis XIV of France. It controlled the lives of enslaved people in French colonies by treating them as property and forcing harsh punishments. Although slavery was abolished in France in 1848, the law itself was never officially removed from French legal books until now.

**What happened** – On May 28, 2026, France’s National Assembly voted 254 to 0 to officially repeal the Code Noir. This means the law is no longer recognized or valid. However, this vote must still be approved by the Senate before it becomes final law. The French President, Emmanuel Macron, has expressed his support for this repeal.

**Impact** – Repealing the Code Noir closes a long-standing legal gap and formally rejects a law that supported slavery. It is a symbolic but important step in recognizing the painful history of slavery in France and its colonies. Lawmakers and activists see this as a moment to remember those affected and to push for further actions, like reparations and addressing ongoing racial inequalities.

**What’s next step** – The Senate will vote on the repeal next. If approved, France will have officially removed its last law related to slavery. Meanwhile, activists and some officials are calling for more to be done, such as programs to fix educational and economic injustices linked to the legacy of slavery.

**One-sentence takeaway** – France’s National Assembly has taken an important step by voting to erase the Code Noir, the last law on slavery, while the country continues to face calls for deeper social change.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Assemblée nationale française vote pour abroger la dernière loi sur l’esclavage, le Code Noir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Code Noir est une loi créée en 1685 par le roi Louis XIV. Elle régissait la vie des personnes esclaves dans les colonies françaises, les traitant comme des biens et imposant des punitions sévères. Bien que l’esclavage ait été aboli en France en 1848, cette loi n’avait jamais été officiellement supprimée jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 28 mai 2026, l’Assemblée nationale française a voté à l’unanimité (254 voix pour, 0 contre) pour abroger officiellement le Code Noir. Cela signifie que cette loi n’est plus reconnue ni valide. Ce vote doit encore être approuvé par le Sénat avant de devenir loi définitive. Le président Emmanuel Macron a exprimé son soutien à cette abrogation.

**Impact** – L’abrogation du Code Noir comble un vide juridique ancien et rejette formellement une loi qui soutenait l’esclavage. C’est une étape symbolique mais importante pour reconnaître l’histoire douloureuse de l’esclavage en France et dans ses colonies. Les députés et les militants voient ce moment comme une occasion de se souvenir des victimes et de réclamer des actions supplémentaires, comme des réparations et la lutte contre les inégalités raciales persistantes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Sénat doit voter sur l’abrogation. Si elle est approuvée, la France aura officiellement supprimé sa dernière loi liée à l’esclavage. Parallèlement, les militants et certains responsables appellent à aller plus loin, avec des programmes pour corriger les injustices éducatives et économiques liées à cet héritage.

**Phrase clé** – L’Assemblée nationale française a franchi une étape importante en votant pour effacer le Code Noir, dernière loi sur l’esclavage, alors que le pays continue de faire face à des appels à un changement social plus profond.
articles.personality_title_es: La Asamblea Nacional de Francia vota para derogar la última ley sobre la esclavitud, el Código Negro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Código Negro fue una ley creada en 1685 por el rey Luis XIV de Francia. Controlaba la vida de las personas esclavizadas en las colonias francesas, tratándolas como propiedad y estableciendo castigos severos. Aunque la esclavitud fue abolida en Francia en 1848, la ley nunca se había eliminado oficialmente hasta ahora.

**Qué sucedió** – El 28 de mayo de 2026, la Asamblea Nacional de Francia votó 254 a 0 para derogar oficialmente el Código Negro. Esto significa que la ley ya no es reconocida ni válida. Esta votación aún debe ser aprobada por el Senado antes de convertirse en ley definitiva. El presidente Emmanuel Macron ha expresado su apoyo a esta derogación.

**Impacto** – La derogación del Código Negro cierra un vacío legal que existía desde hace mucho tiempo y rechaza formalmente una ley que apoyaba la esclavitud. Es un paso simbólico pero importante para reconocer la dolorosa historia de la esclavitud en Francia y sus colonias. Legisladores y activistas ven este momento como una oportunidad para recordar a las víctimas y pedir acciones adicionales, como reparaciones y la lucha contra las desigualdades raciales.

**Próximo paso** – El Senado votará sobre la derogación próximamente. Si la aprueba, Francia habrá eliminado oficialmente su última ley relacionada con la esclavitud. Mientras tanto, activistas y algunos funcionarios piden que se haga más para corregir injusticias educativas y económicas vinculadas al legado de la esclavitud.

**Frase clave** – La Asamblea Nacional de Francia ha dado un paso importante al votar para borrar el Código Negro, la última ley sobre la esclavitud, mientras el país sigue enfrentando llamados a un cambio social más profundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, ancient book titled “Code Noir” gently closing on a wooden table, surrounded by soft candlelight, with symbolic chains breaking apart beside it and a distant view of a calm Caribbean shoreline under a peaceful sky, rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: Marking historic progress on rights for persons with disabilities, UN conference tackles critical gaps
articles.summary: Historical gains have been hard-won, but much more needs to be done to advance progress in realising promises made two decades ago, said the UN chief at the opening on Tuesday of the 19th global meeting on the rights of persons with disabilities at UN Headquarters.
articles.content:
“The world benefits when everyone has the chance to use their skills in the service of humanity and to pursue their dreams,” UN Secretary-General António Guterres told the opening session held in the iconic General Assembly Hall. “Your discussions today are a vital next step towards a world where all of us are respected and included.”

Tweet URL

Known as COSP19, this year’s the Conference of State Parties to the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities runs from 9 to 11 June under the theme Celebrating and consolidating achievements and shaping the next phase of implementation in a changing world, with roundtable discussions, elections, decisions on best ways forward and first-hand accounts of challenges, innovative solutions and success stories from around the world.

‘While progress is real, it is unacceptably slow’

Actions hinge on the convention, CRPD, a legally-binding agreement between Member State signatories to uphold, promote and protect the rights of persons with disabilities, adopted by the UN General Assembly in 2006 and now ratified by 192 countries.

Twenty years later, much has been accomplished, including that over 90 per cent of countries have laws guaranteeing the rights of persons with disabilities, but much more must be done in light of the latest UN Disability and Development Report findings that show almost all of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) indicators for persons with disabilities are off track.

“While progress is real, it is unacceptably slow,” the UN chief said.

‘Luckily, it is possible to redesign the world’

Pointing to a world facing crises, from climate change and conflict to a global surge in the cost of living, Mr. Guterres said “too often, living with a disability means living in a world designed by and for others. Luckily, it is possible to redesign the world.”

Striving to lead by example, the UN’s Disability Inclusion Strategy aims to ensure change, led by the insights of people with disabilities.

“Together, let’s realise the rights of every person with disabilities and build a fair and vibrant future for all.”

Watch the opening session here:

Civil society voices heard

Communities from around the world have already amplified their voices on Monday, holding a multi-session Civil Society Forum under the auspices of COSP19 to tackle pressing issues, from building resilient societies to enhancing accessible civic engagement, leadership and advocacy in political and public life.

In case you missed it, tune in to UN Web TV at the links below to access these forum sessions:

Find out more about this year’s forum here.

COSP19: Explained COSP meetings have been held annually at UN Headquarters in New York since 2008, covering a range of themes and issues

Click here for maps of UN Headquarters, including its Accessibility Centre and Kiosk

Get full access to COSP19 on UN Web TV live or on demand with closed captioning and sign language interpretation, including dozens of side events

Read the concept note here

Find the draft programme here and an easy read version here

Engage on social media with #COSP19 #CRPD20

Live page takeover: From 2024

Check out UN News’ first live blog takeover by activist, actor and talk show host Nick Herd, who was our guest editor, sharing his story and journey throughout the opening day of the 17th session, here.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on significant global progress and ongoing efforts to advance the rights of persons with disabilities through a major UN conference. It highlights a legally binding international convention ratified by 192 countries, broad societal impact, and concrete actions aimed at inclusion and equality. The story is focused, detailed, and emphasizes both achievements and the need for continued progress, making it inspiring and hopeful for a large population worldwide.
articles.personality_title: UN conference highlights progress and new steps for disability rights worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since 2006, the United Nations has a global agreement called the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD). It promises to protect and promote the rights of people with disabilities. Almost every country in the world has agreed to follow this convention.

**What happened** – From June 9 to 11, the 19th Conference of State Parties (COSP19) met at the UN Headquarters in New York. Leaders, activists, and experts gathered to discuss what has been achieved and what still needs to be done to improve the lives of persons with disabilities. The conference featured talks, elections, and shared stories from around the world.

**Impact** – The conference showed that while many countries now have laws to protect disability rights, real progress is slow. The UN Secretary-General António Guterres pointed out that people with disabilities often live in a world made for others, but it is possible to change this. The event also included a special forum where civil society groups shared ideas for building fairer and more accessible communities.

**What's next step** – The UN is working on a Disability Inclusion Strategy to make sure that people with disabilities lead the way in shaping a fairer future. The conference’s discussions and decisions will guide countries on how to better include persons with disabilities in all parts of life. The event will be followed by ongoing activities and updates shared online.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s global meeting on disability rights brought together countries and communities to celebrate progress and plan stronger actions for a more inclusive world.
articles.personality_title_fr: La conférence de l’ONU souligne les progrès et les prochaines étapes pour les droits des personnes handicapées dans le monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 2006, les Nations Unies ont une convention mondiale appelée la Convention relative aux droits des personnes handicapées (CDPH). Elle promet de protéger et de promouvoir les droits des personnes handicapées. Presque tous les pays du monde ont accepté de suivre cette convention.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Du 9 au 11 juin, la 19e Conférence des États parties (COSP19) s’est tenue au siège de l’ONU à New York. Des dirigeants, des militants et des experts se sont réunis pour discuter des réalisations et des actions encore nécessaires pour améliorer la vie des personnes handicapées. La conférence comprenait des discussions, des élections et des témoignages venus du monde entier.

**Impact** – La conférence a montré que, bien que de nombreux pays aient désormais des lois protégeant les droits des personnes handicapées, les progrès restent lents. Le secrétaire général de l’ONU, António Guterres, a souligné que les personnes handicapées vivent souvent dans un monde conçu pour les autres, mais qu’il est possible de changer cela. Un forum spécial a aussi permis aux groupes de la société civile de partager des idées pour construire des communautés plus justes et accessibles.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ONU travaille sur une stratégie d’inclusion des personnes handicapées pour s’assurer qu’elles participent pleinement à la construction d’un avenir équitable. Les discussions et décisions de la conférence guideront les pays pour mieux inclure les personnes handicapées dans tous les aspects de la vie. L’événement sera suivi par des activités continues et des mises à jour en ligne.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La réunion mondiale de l’ONU sur les droits des personnes handicapées a réuni pays et communautés pour célébrer les progrès et planifier des actions plus fortes pour un monde plus inclusif.
articles.personality_title_es: Conferencia de la ONU destaca avances y próximos pasos para los derechos de las personas con discapacidad en el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde 2006, las Naciones Unidas tienen un acuerdo global llamado la Convención sobre los Derechos de las Personas con Discapacidad (CDPD). Este acuerdo promete proteger y promover los derechos de las personas con discapacidad. Casi todos los países del mundo han aceptado seguir esta convención.

**Qué pasó** – Del 9 al 11 de junio, se llevó a cabo en la sede de la ONU en Nueva York la 19ª Conferencia de los Estados Partes (COSP19). Líderes, activistas y expertos se reunieron para hablar sobre lo que se ha logrado y lo que aún falta para mejorar la vida de las personas con discapacidad. La conferencia incluyó debates, elecciones y relatos de experiencias de todo el mundo.

**Impacto** – La conferencia mostró que, aunque muchos países tienen leyes para proteger los derechos de las personas con discapacidad, el progreso es lento. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, señaló que las personas con discapacidad a menudo viven en un mundo diseñado para otros, pero que es posible cambiar esto. También hubo un foro especial donde grupos de la sociedad civil compartieron ideas para construir comunidades más justas y accesibles.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU está trabajando en una Estrategia de Inclusión para asegurar que las personas con discapacidad lideren la construcción de un futuro más justo. Las discusiones y decisiones de la conferencia guiarán a los países para incluir mejor a las personas con discapacidad en todos los ámbitos de la vida. El evento continuará con actividades y actualizaciones en línea.

**Resumen en una frase** – La reunión global de la ONU sobre derechos de discapacidad reunió a países y comunidades para celebrar avances y planear acciones más fuertes hacia un mundo más inclusivo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an inclusive roundtable formed by interconnected hands of diverse shapes and sizes, some with assistive devices like a wheelchair wheel and a white cane subtly integrated into the composition, set in a softly lit, symbolic assembly hall with abstract architectural elements inspired by the UN General Assembly, using natural and simple earthy tones.

articles.title: World News in Brief: Call for action against child labour, ICC Prosecutor suspended, WFP raises awareness in Egypt
articles.summary: Ahead of World Day Against Child Labour on 12 June, the UN is urging governments and communities to accelerate efforts to end a crisis that still affects millions of children worldwide.
articles.content:
Today, nearly 138 million children remain trapped in child labour globally, including 54 million engaged in hazardous work that threatens their health, safety and development.

“This is unacceptable – we must accelerate action,” said Gilbert F. Houngbo, Director-General of the International Labour Organization, ILO, in a video message.

He called for renewed global commitment through expanded access to decent work, education, social protection and stronger legal safeguards.

ILO Video | 138 million children in child labour.

Ensuring progress

This year’s theme, Red Card to Child Labour: Fair play for children, decent work for adults, highlights the need to ensure every child can learn, play and grow up in a safe environment.

The recently adopted Marrakech Global Framework for Action against Child Labour aims to provide a roadmap to reach that goal, focusing on decent jobs for parents, universal access to quality education, stronger social protection systems and reinforced laws and institutions.

For the first time, the framework also introduces measurable indicators and an accountability mechanism designed to track implementation and maintain momentum, beyond the 2030 target date.

“Together, let us act with urgency and determination to end child labour,” Mr. Houngbo urged.

ICC chief prosecutor suspended as investigations into sexual misconduct continue

The International Criminal Court’s Prosecutor Karim Khan has been suspended “with immediate effect” following an investigation into allegations of sexual misconduct made against him.

In a statement on Tuesday, the ICC President’s office confirmed the decision of its executive committee to refer the disciplinary proceedings against the prosecutor to the court’s 125 Member States, pending a final decision.

Mr. Khan has repeatedly denied the allegations, which first emerged in 2024 when multiple allegations relating to a female colleague at the Hague were reported, involving the prominent British lawyer.

Tweet URL

The ICC's governing body will send the conclusion reached by its internal investigation on to members, who will decide Mr. Khan’s future in a special session convened at a later date. The body also stressed the suspension does not prejudge the outcome of the case.

“Fostering the dignity, rights and aspirations of the court’s personnel are key priorities of the court’s leadership,” the President’s office said.

The International Criminal Court is an independent judicial body established under the Rome Statute, adopted in 1998 and in force since 2002. Although not part of the United Nations, the ICC works closely with it under a cooperative framework to investigate and prosecute most serious crimes.

WFP brings food security to the big screen in Egypt

The UN World Food Programme (WFP) is turning cinema screens across Egypt into a platform for raising awareness about hunger and food security through a new campaign titled Not My Day.

Ahead of movie showings nationwide, the campaign uses storytelling to spotlight the frequently overlooked role food security plays in shaping everyday life.

Developed in partnership with the Union of Video and Filmmakers and creative production hub Tayarah, the promotional video reaches audiences in cinemas across the country.

Running across 46 cinema screens until 17 June, the campaign comes at a time when funding shortages continue to threaten critical food assistance programmes.

Familiar faces

The campaign features well-known Egyptian public figures, including actor Ahmed Magdy, iEvents founder Amr Mansi, chef and culinary academy founder Mona El Banna, award-winning chef Mostafa Seif and chef Peri Saleh.

Each appears to describe their own daily routines, before audiences discover they are instead telling the real experiences of people supported by WFP in Egypt.

By placing familiar voices behind stories of food insecurity, the campaign highlights how access to food underpins dignity, stability and opportunity.

Through the campaign, the agency is “engaging new audiences in a conversation about food security in a way never done before,” said Rawad Halabi, WFP Country Director and Representative in Egypt.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant global issue—child labour affecting 138 million children—and reports on renewed international commitment and a new global framework to end child labour with measurable indicators and accountability, which has broad positive societal impact. The other parts of the article (ICC suspension and WFP campaign) are either neutral or less impactful, so the main good news is the child labour action.
articles.personality_title: UN launches new plan to end child labour affecting 138 million children
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – About 138 million children around the world are still forced to work, often in dangerous jobs that harm their health and growth. This problem is called child labour.

**What happened** – The United Nations and the International Labour Organization (ILO) have introduced a new plan called the Marrakech Global Framework for Action. It was announced just before World Day Against Child Labour on June 12. The plan aims to help end child labour by improving jobs for parents, giving all children access to good education, and making laws stronger to protect children.

**Impact** – This plan is special because it sets clear goals and ways to measure progress. For the first time, it includes rules to check if countries are doing their part to stop child labour. This helps keep governments accountable and focused on protecting children’s rights.

**What's next step** – Countries around the world will work together using this plan. They will try to create more decent jobs for adults and better schools for children. The plan will be checked regularly to make sure it is working, aiming for big improvements by 2030 and beyond.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new global plan sets clear steps and checks to help end child labour and protect millions of children’s futures worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance un nouveau plan pour mettre fin au travail des enfants touchant 138 millions d’enfants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Environ 138 millions d’enfants dans le monde sont encore forcés de travailler, souvent dans des emplois dangereux qui nuisent à leur santé et à leur développement. Ce problème s’appelle le travail des enfants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les Nations Unies et l’Organisation internationale du Travail (OIT) ont présenté un nouveau plan appelé le Cadre mondial de Marrakech pour l’action. Il a été annoncé juste avant la Journée mondiale contre le travail des enfants, le 12 juin. Le plan vise à mettre fin au travail des enfants en améliorant les emplois pour les parents, en donnant à tous les enfants accès à une bonne éducation, et en renforçant les lois pour protéger les enfants.

**Impact** – Ce plan est spécial car il fixe des objectifs clairs et des moyens de mesurer les progrès. Pour la première fois, il inclut des règles pour vérifier si les pays font leur part pour arrêter le travail des enfants. Cela aide à rendre les gouvernements responsables et concentrés sur la protection des droits des enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays du monde entier vont travailler ensemble avec ce plan. Ils essaieront de créer plus d’emplois décents pour les adultes et de meilleures écoles pour les enfants. Le plan sera régulièrement contrôlé pour s’assurer qu’il fonctionne, avec pour objectif des améliorations importantes d’ici 2030 et au-delà.

**Phrase clé** – Le nouveau plan mondial de l’ONU fixe des étapes claires et des contrôles pour aider à mettre fin au travail des enfants et protéger l’avenir de millions d’enfants dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU lanza un nuevo plan para acabar con el trabajo infantil que afecta a 138 millones de niños
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cerca de 138 millones de niños en el mundo todavía son obligados a trabajar, a menudo en trabajos peligrosos que dañan su salud y desarrollo. Este problema se llama trabajo infantil.

**Qué pasó** – Las Naciones Unidas y la Organización Internacional del Trabajo (OIT) presentaron un nuevo plan llamado el Marco Global de Marrakech para la Acción. Fue anunciado justo antes del Día Mundial contra el Trabajo Infantil, el 12 de junio. El plan busca acabar con el trabajo infantil mejorando los empleos para los padres, dando a todos los niños acceso a buena educación y fortaleciendo las leyes para proteger a los niños.

**Impacto** – Este plan es especial porque establece metas claras y formas de medir el progreso. Por primera vez, incluye reglas para verificar si los países están cumpliendo para detener el trabajo infantil. Esto ayuda a que los gobiernos sean responsables y se concentren en proteger los derechos de los niños.

**Próximo paso** – Los países del mundo trabajarán juntos usando este plan. Intentarán crear más empleos decentes para adultos y mejores escuelas para los niños. El plan será revisado regularmente para asegurar que funcione, con el objetivo de grandes mejoras para 2030 y más allá.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo plan global de la ONU establece pasos claros y controles para ayudar a acabar con el trabajo infantil y proteger el futuro de millones de niños en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, open book symbolizing education, resting on a sturdy pair of adult hands representing decent work and protection, with a gentle red card hovering above to signify a call to end child labour, set against a soft, natural palette of earth tones and muted reds.
